---
title: "MO850 – Tópicos Avançados em Ciência da Computação I"
format:
  revealjs:
    scrollable: true
    incremental: true
    width: 1500
    slide-number: c/t
editor: visual
---

## Requirements

R:<https://cran.r-project.org/>

RStudio (IDE): <https://posit.co/downloads/>

## R Crash Course {.incremental}

-   GUI
-   Projects
-   Variables
-   Functions
-   Installing and loading packages
-   the "tidyverse"
-   importing datasets

## Data exploration

Once the dataset is imported, we can `View` it.

::: fragment
```{r}
#| echo: true
library(readr)
raw_data <- read_csv("data/timebin_sampled_telegram.csv")
head(raw_data)
```
:::

## Introduction to Quanteda

`quanteda` is an R package for managing and analyzing text^\[1\]^

::: fragment
Beyond the base package, `quanteda` offers a plethora of specialized extensions, for example:

-   `quanteda.textstats`: Metrics for similarity, readability, lexical diversity, and more
-   `quanteda.textplots`: Plotting and visualising text data
-   `quanteda.dictionaries`: Dictionaries for word counting applications
-   `quanteda.sentiment`: Tools for advanced dictionary-based sentiment analysis
:::

::: fragment
`quanteda` is also [well documented](https://tutorials.quanteda.io/).
:::

## The quanteda pipeline

The common `quanteda` pipeline is: corpus -\> tokens -\> DFM

::: fragment
Tokenization = splitting a long text into smaller pieces (i.e., tokens).
:::

::: fragment
Different levels of tokenization:

-   words
-   stems
-   lemmas
-   n-grams
-   shingles
:::

::: fragment
Document-feature matrix (DFM): a table where each row is a document (i.e. a text in our corpus) and each column is a feature (usually a token). Values represent how many times the feature appears in a document *d*
:::

## Open vocabulary word counting

Open vocabulary vs. dictionaries = bottom-up vs top-down

::: fragment
It all starts with raw frequency counts.
:::

## Text normalization

Occurrence threshold: removing features below or above a term or doc frequency threshold.

-   cleaner results
-   threshold are hard to justify

::: fragment
Stopwords: high-frequency words that carry little **semantic** meaning (e.g., "and", "or"). We can create custom stopwords.

-   intuitive
-   remove potential important information^\[2\]^
:::

## Transforming word counts: Boolean tokenization

Boolean (binary) tokenization: sometimes first occurrence is more important than frequency.

-   removes non-independence of observations
-   devalues common tokens
-   text length confound

## Transforming word counts: Relative tokenization

Relative tokenization (or frequency): word counts / total words

-   intuitive appeal
-   discounts longer texts
-   does not control for text length
-   not normally distributed

## Transforming word counts: Term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF)

TF-IDF: How representative is that token of the particular features of that document as opposed to other documents?

::: fragment
$$TF(t,d) = \frac{\text{number of times } t \text{ appears in } d}{\text{total number of words in } d}$$

$$IDF(t,D) = \log \frac{\text{total number of documents in } D}{\text{number of documents containing } t}$$
:::

-   computational efficient
-   discount uninformative tokens with no information loss
-   difficult to interpret directly
-   we might be interested in common tokens across docs

## Comparing texts with "keyness"

Frequency ratios are statistically misleading

::: fragment
Keyness helps us comparing groups.
:::

::: fragment
However, we can also compare continuous variables (e.g., measures)
:::

## Topic Modeling: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)

Unsupervised method to discover latent *topics* in a corpus — no predefined groups needed.

$$P(w | d) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} P(w | topic_k) \cdot P(topic_k | d)$$

::: fragment
Two key ideas:

-   A **topic** is a probability distribution over words
-   A **document** is a mixture of topics
:::

## References

1)  Benoit, Kenneth, Kohei Watanabe, Haiyan Wang, Paul Nulty, Adam Obeng, Stefan Müller, and Akitaka Matsuo. 2018. “quanteda: An R package for the quantitative analysis of textual data”. Journal of Open Source Software 3(30), 774. https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.00774.

2)  Mehl, M. R., Gosling, S. D., & Pennebaker, J. W. (2006). Personality in its natural habitat: Manifestations and implicit folk theories of personality in daily life. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 90 5, 862–877. https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:2932332
